nameMeanings.name: ab
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  African/Muslim Amadou, Ousmane, Demba, Mamadou, Daouda, Ibrahima, Mohamed, Thierno, Abdoul, Abdoulaye, Abou, Aissatou, Fatime, Fatou, Mame, Moussa, Oumar, Yassin.  Vietnamese Dung, Thai Van, Tho, Thuy, Toan, Tong Van, Tran.
West African (mainly Senegal and Mauritania): Fulani name, from the Mandinka adjective ba(a) ‘big, great’. Compare Bah 1, see also Balde 1.
Muslim: abstracted as a surname from bā, a dialect form of Arabic Abu ‘father’, used in compound surnames such as Bā Wazīr and Bā Ṭarafi.

variants.name: Balde
meanings: West African (mainly Senegal and Mauritania): Fulani name, regarded as an equivalent (variant) of   Ba   1.
German: from a short form of an ancient Germanic personal name formed with bald ‘bold, quick’ (see Bald ).
Catalan (Baldé): possibly from an old personal name of ancient Germanic origin (see 2 above).

variants.name: Bah
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  African/Muslim Mamadou, Thierno, Amadou, Boubacar, Fatmata, Fatoumata, Mariama, Oumou, Umaru, Aboubacar, Aissatou, Alieu, Mohamed, Abdulai, Ibrahima, Abdul, Abdoul, Ibrahim, Abou, Abu, Abubakar, Ali, Hassane, Mahmoud.
West African (mainly Guinea, also common in the Gambia and Sierra Leone): variant of the Fulani name   Ba   1.
Slovenian: probably a nickname from a derivative of bahati se ‘to boast, to pride oneself’. Compare Bach 7.

nameMeanings.name: Ionas
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: Americanized form of German, Polish or Sorbian Jonas , Czech or Slovak Jonáš, Hungarian Jónás.

Ethiopian: from the personal name Yonas, Ethiopian form of Jonas . — Note: Since Ethiopians do not have hereditary surnames, this name was registered as such only after immigration of its bearers to the US.

variants.name: Jonas
meanings: English, German, French, Dutch, Polish, Lithuanian, Sorbian, and Jewish (Ashkenazic); Czech (Jonáš); Slovak (mainly Jonáš, also Jónáš, Jónás); Hungarian (Jónás): from a personal name, which is a variant of the Biblical name Jonah, Hebrew Yonah, meaning ‘dove’ in Hebrew. In the book of the Bible which bears his name, Jonah was appointed by God to preach repentance to the city of Nineveh, but tried to flee instead to Tarshish. On the voyage to Tarshish, a great storm arose and Jonah was thrown overboard by his shipmates to appease God's wrath, swallowed by a great fish, and delivered by it on the shores of Nineveh. This story exercised a powerful hold on the popular imagination in medieval Europe, and the personal name was a relatively common choice. The Hebrew name and its reflexes in other languages (for example Yiddish Yoyne) have been popular Jewish personal names for generations. In North America, this surname is also an altered form of the Polish variant Jonasz. Compare Yonas .

nameMeanings.name: Ynyr
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of First Names © Patrick Hanks and Flavia Hodges 1990, 2003, 2006.
meanings: Traditional: pronounced ‘inn-eer’, probably from the Latin name Honorius (a derivative of honor ‘renown’).
